content.text:
Task Prompt
Implement a GreenLeaf Operations Suite for an offline-first plant-care retailer and greenhouse operator, covering day-to-day procurement plus internal knowledge sharing in one React-based web UI. Users sign in with a locally managed username and password and land on a role-based workspace for Procurement Managers, Warehouse Clerks, Plant Care Specialists, and Administrators; suppliers can be given limited portal access on shared workstations for viewing purchase orders and return authorizations. The procurement flow supports purchase-demand creation from low-stock alerts or manual requests, an approval chain with dollar thresholds (auto-approve up to $500.00, supervisor approval up to $5,000.00, and dual approval beyond that), and a closed loop from approved request to purchase order, receiving, and putaway. Receiving screens guide barcode/manual entry, require variance reason codes for short/over/damaged items, and generate returns with a default 14-day return window and configurable restocking fee rules. A Plant Care knowledge base provides structured articles (care guides, pest treatment SOPs, safety notes) with version history, change summaries, favorites, and offline cache on the device; Administrators can run phased releases so drafts are visible only to Specialists until promoted to ΓÇ£Storewide.ΓÇ¥ Intelligent search supports tags, synonyms, ΓÇ£similar articlesΓÇ¥ suggestions, and saved search history. A unified in-app notification center surfaces event-driven messages such as request approval, schedule changes, cancellations, and review outcomes, with read receipts, per-user subscription preferences, and throttling (no more than 20 notifications per user per hour) to prevent alert fatigue. The backend uses NestJS to expose RESTful APIs consumed by the React frontend, enforcing server-side validation, role permissions, and transactional integrity, with PostgreSQL as the system of record for suppliers, payment terms, requests, purchase orders, receipts, putaway locations, knowledge articles, notifications, and audit trails. Supplier profiles store payment terms (for example Net 30, 2/10 Net 30) and maintain a local funds ledger for recording deposits, escrow holds, and refunds as accounting events; third-party payment connectors remain pluggable but are disabled by default to keep the system fully functional without internet access, and all callbacks are handled idempotently when enabled in a controlled on-prem environment. Entity deduplication and ID alignment prevent duplicate catalog and knowledge records by generating fingerprints (normalized title, supplier, unit size, and optional UPC) and applying fuzzy matching thresholds (flag matches at 0.90 similarity for review, auto-merge at 0.97 with admin approval), maintaining mapping tables so historical reports and training datasets arenΓÇÖt contaminated by duplicates. Data quality and observability run locally: scheduled integrity checks detect missing required fields, duplicate suppliers, and outlier pricing (over 3├ù median), while trace logs, job duration metrics, queue length, and resource usage are available in an admin dashboard; failed jobs retry up to three times with exponential backoff and automatically roll back partial writes via database transactions. Sensitive fields (password hashes, supplier banking notes, internal risk flags) are encrypted or masked at rest, passwords are hashed with bcrypt, sessions expire after 30 minutes of inactivity, and the entire deployment is designed to run on a single machine or local LAN with no external dependencies.
Non-front-end testing prompt words
You are the reviewer responsible for Delivery Acceptance and Project Architecture Audit.
In the current working directory, review the project against the Business / Task Prompt and the Acceptance / Scoring Criteria.
[Acceptance / Scoring Criteria (the only authority)]
{
1. Hard Gates
1.1 Whether the delivered project can actually be run and verified
- Whether clear startup or run instructions are provided
- Whether the project can be started or run without modifying core code
- Whether the actual runtime behavior is broadly consistent with the delivery documentation
1.2 Whether the delivered project materially deviates from the Prompt
- Whether the implementation is centered on the business goal or usage scenario described in the Prompt
- Whether there are major parts of the implementation that are only loosely related, or unrelated, to the Prompt
- Whether the project replaces, weakens, or ignores the core problem definition in the Prompt without justification
2. Delivery Completeness
2.1 Whether the delivered project fully covers the core requirements explicitly stated in the Prompt
- Whether all explicitly stated core functional requirements in the Prompt are implemented
2.2 Whether the delivered project represents a basic end-to-end deliverable from 0 to 1, rather than a partial feature, illustrative implementation, or code fragment
- Whether mock / hardcoded behavior is used in place of real logic without explanation
- Whether the project includes a complete project structure rather than scattered code or a single-file example
- Whether basic project documentation is provided, such as a README or equivalent
3. Engineering and Architecture Quality
3.1 Whether the project adopts a reasonable engineering structure and module decomposition for the scale of the problem
- Whether the project structure is clear and module responsibilities are reasonably defined
- Whether the project contains redundant or unnecessary files
- Whether the implementation is excessively piled into a single file
3.2 Whether the project shows basic maintainability and extensibility, rather than being a temporary or stacked implementation
- Whether there are obvious signs of chaotic structure or tight coupling
- Whether the core logic leaves room for extension rather than being completely hard-coded
4. Engineering Details and Professionalism
4.1 Whether the engineering details and overall shape reflect professional software practice, including but not limited to error handling, logging, validation, and API design
- Whether error handling is basically reliable and user-friendly
- Whether logs support troubleshooting rather than being random print statements or completely absent
- Whether necessary validation is present for key inputs and boundary conditions
4.2 Whether the project is organized like a real product or service, rather than remaining at the level of an example or demo
- Whether the overall deliverable resembles a real application instead of a teaching sample or demonstration-only project
5. Prompt Understanding and Requirement Fit
5.1 Whether the project accurately understands and responds to the business goal, usage scenario, and implicit constraints described in the Prompt, rather than merely implementing surface-level technical features
- Whether the core business objective in the Prompt is implemented correctly
- Whether there are obvious misunderstandings of the requirement semantics or deviations from the actual problem
- Whether key constraints in the Prompt are changed or ignored without explanation
6. Aesthetics (frontend-only / full-stack tasks only)
6.1 Whether the visual and interaction design fits the scenario and demonstrates reasonable visual quality
- Whether different functional areas of the page are visually distinguishable through background, spacing, separation, or hierarchy
- Whether the overall layout is reasonable, and whether alignment, spacing, and proportions are broadly consistent
- Whether UI elements, including text, images, and icons, render and display correctly
- Whether visual elements are consistent with the page theme and textual content, and whether there are obvious mismatches between images, illustrations, decorative elements, and the actual content
- Whether basic interaction feedback is provided, such as hover states, click states, or transitions, so users can understand the current interaction state
- Whether fonts, font sizes, colors, and icon styles are generally consistent, without obvious visual inconsistency or mixed design language
}
Review Objective
Determine whether the delivered project is a credible, runnable, prompt-aligned, and minimally professional 0-to-1 deliverable.
Priority Order
1. delivery runnability boundary
2. prompt requirement fit
3. security-critical flaws
4. test sufficiency
5. major engineering quality issues
6. frontend aesthetics only if clearly applicable
Execution Rules
1) Review only the highest-impact findings that can change the final verdict.
Do not perform exhaustive enumeration of every secondary or tertiary checklist item.
2) Do not relax standards for:
- security
- prompt-fit
- delivery completeness
- test sufficiency
- evidence for material conclusions
3) Do not skip any issue that could independently cause a Fail or Partial Pass verdict.
4) If a security, prompt-fit, runnability, or core test-sufficiency issue is suspected, continue investigation until it is either evidenced or explicitly marked Cannot Confirm.
5) Stop after either:
- identifying up to 10 findings total, or
- identifying up to 5 High / Blocker findings,
whichever comes first.
6) Do not modify project code.
7) Use evidence only for material conclusions.
For any conclusion that changes the final verdict, provide concrete, traceable evidence using file path + line number, tool output, or explicit runtime result.
8) If evidence is insufficient, do not guess.
Use "Cannot Confirm" or explicitly label the assumption and its boundary.
9) Perform runtime verification only when all of the following are true:
- the command is explicitly documented
- no Docker is required
- no Docker-related command is required
- no container orchestration is required
- no privileged system access is required
- no external network / third-party dependency is required
- expected completion is short
10) Never run Docker-related commands.
This includes, but is not limited to:
- docker
- docker compose
- docker-compose
- podman
- container runtime / orchestration commands with equivalent effect
11) If verification would require Docker or any container-related command, do not execute it.
Instead:
- state that Docker-based runtime verification was not performed
- treat it as a verification boundary, not automatically as a project defect
- provide the local reproduction command(s) the user can run
- state what was confirmed statically
- state what remains unconfirmed
12) Docker non-execution is a verification constraint, not a project defect by itself.
Only report a defect if the project itself lacks runnable documentation, has broken setup logic, or shows static evidence of delivery failure.
13) Security review has priority over style issues.
Always assess:
- authentication entry points
- route-level authorization
- object-level authorization
- tenant / user isolation
Expand security review further only if relevant code paths exist, such as:
- admin / internal / debug endpoints
- function-level authorization
- privilege escalation paths
14) Test review is mandatory, but do not build a full requirement-to-test traceability matrix.
Assess only whether tests sufficiently cover:
- the core business happy path
- major failure paths such as validation failure, 401, 403, 404, 409 where relevant
- security-critical areas
- obvious high-risk boundaries directly relevant to the business flow
15) For test coverage, state only:
- covered / partially covered / missing / cannot confirm
- one or two supporting evidence points
- the minimum additional test needed if coverage is weak
16) Logging review is mandatory but concise.
Assess only:
- whether logging exists for meaningful troubleshooting
- whether logging categories are reasonably clear if present
- whether there is obvious sensitive-data leakage risk in logs or responses
17) Mock / stub / fake behavior is not a defect by itself unless the Prompt or documentation requires real integration.
If present, explain only:
- the mock scope
- how it is enabled
- whether there is obvious accidental production-use risk
18) Do not continue searching for additional low-severity issues after the final verdict is already supportable.
19) Do not read unrelated files once enough evidence has been collected to support the verdict and top findings.
Required Output Format
Return exactly these sections:
1. Verdict
- Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm
2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- what was reviewed
- what was not executed
- whether Docker-based verification was required but not executed
- what remains unconfirmed
3. Top Findings
- up to 10 findings only
- each finding must include:
  - Severity: Blocker / High / Medium / Low
  - Conclusion
  - Brief rationale
  - Evidence
  - Impact
  - Minimum actionable fix
4. Security Summary
- authentication
- route authorization
- object-level authorization
- tenant / user isolation
For each, return:
- Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm
- brief evidence or verification boundary
5. Test Sufficiency Summary
Return:
- Test Overview
  - whether unit tests exist
  - whether API / integration tests exist
  - obvious test entry points if present
- Core Coverage
  - happy path: covered / partial / missing / cannot confirm
  - key failure paths: covered / partial / missing / cannot confirm
  - security-critical coverage: covered / partial / missing / cannot confirm
- Major Gaps
  - up to 3 highest-risk missing tests
- Final Test Verdict
  - Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm
6. Engineering Quality Summary
Assess only major maintainability / architecture concerns that materially affect delivery confidence.
7. Next Actions
- up to 5 minimum actions only
- prioritize by severity and unblock value
Final Verification Before Output
Before finalizing, check all of the following:
1. Does each material conclusion have supporting evidence?
2. Are any claims stronger than the evidence supports?
3. If unsupported observations are removed, does the final verdict still hold?
4. Has any uncertain point been incorrectly presented as a confirmed fact?
5. Has security or test sufficiency been judged too loosely without evidence?
6. Has any Docker non-execution boundary been incorrectly described as a confirmed runtime failure?
If file writing is supported, save the final report to a markdown file.
Otherwise, return the report in-chat.
Front-end testing prompt words
You are the reviewer responsible for ΓÇ£Delivery Acceptance / Project Architecture Inspection.ΓÇ¥
In the current working directory, review the frontend project point by point and make determination judgments based on the [Business / Task Prompt] and the [Acceptance / Scoring Criteria]. The acceptance criteria are the sole standard of judgment.
[Acceptance / Scoring Criteria (single source of truth)]
{
1. Mandatory Gate Checks
1.1 Can the delivered project actually be run and verified?
- Is there a clear explanation of how to start, run, build, or preview the project?
- Can it be started, built, or verified locally without modifying core code?
- Do the actual results generally match the delivery documentation?
1.2 Does the deliverable materially deviate from the Prompt?
- Does the implementation stay aligned with the business goal, page scenarios, and user flows described in the Prompt?
- Is there functionality that is only weakly related or unrelated to the Prompt?
- Has the implementation replaced, weakened, or ignored the core problem definition in the Prompt without explanation?
2. Completeness of Delivery
2.1 Does the deliverable fully cover the core requirements explicitly stated in the Prompt?
- Are the required pages, core features, core interactions, and key UI states implemented?
- Are the main user flows covered, rather than only static UI or isolated fragments?
2.2 Does the deliverable have the shape of a real end-to-end project rather than a partial sample, demo fragment, or illustrative code snippet?
- Is mock / hardcoded behavior used in place of real logic without being disclosed?
- Is there a complete project structure rather than scattered code or a single-file example?
- Is there basic project documentation such as a README or equivalent?
- Does the project have a basic organization for pages, routing, state, or data flow, rather than just stitched-together display code?
3. Engineering and Architecture Quality
3.1 Does the deliverable use a reasonable structure and module split for the scope of the problem?
- Is the project structure clear, with reasonably separated responsibilities?
- Is there basic separation across pages, components, state, service calls, and utility functions?
- Are there unnecessary or redundant files?
- Is too much logic stacked into a single file?
3.2 Does the deliverable show basic maintainability and extensibility rather than being a temporary or piled-up implementation?
- Is there obvious confusion or tight coupling?
- Does the core logic leave room for extension, or is everything hardcoded?
- Are component reuse, state management, API abstraction, and constant/config organization handled in a maintainable way?
4. Engineering Detail and Professionalism
4.1 Does the deliverable reflect sound frontend engineering practice in terms of error handling, logging, validation, state feedback, and interaction design?
- Is error handling basically reliable and user-friendly?
- Is necessary validation present for important inputs, key interactions, and boundary cases?
- Are essential UI states handled, such as loading, empty, error, submitting, and success / failure feedback?
- Is logging used to support troubleshooting rather than being random, excessive, or entirely absent?
- Is there any risk of sensitive data being exposed through console output, analytics, visible UI content, or similar surfaces?
4.2 Does the deliverable resemble a real product rather than a demo or tutorial artifact?
- Does the project look like a real application rather than a teaching sample or showcase demo?
- Are the pages meaningfully connected to each other?
- Are the interaction flows complete, rather than only displaying static outcomes?
5. Prompt Understanding and Fit
5.1 Does the deliverable correctly understand and respond to the business goal, usage scenario, and implied constraints in the Prompt, rather than merely implementing surface-level UI?
- Does it correctly fulfill the PromptΓÇÖs core business objective?
- Is there any clear misunderstanding of the requirement or deviation from the real problem being solved?
- Have key constraints in the Prompt been changed or ignored without explanation?
- Does the project only ΓÇ£look rightΓÇ¥ visually while failing to complete the actual interaction flow, state transitions, or user task closure?
6. Visual and Interaction Quality (frontend projects only)
6.1 Are the visuals and interactions appropriate to the scenario, and is the design reasonably polished?
- Are different functional areas visually distinguishable through background, separation, spacing, hierarchy, or similar means?
- Is the overall layout coherent, with consistent alignment, spacing, and proportions?
- Do UI elements such as text, images, and icons render correctly?
- Do the visual elements match the theme and content, or are there images / illustrations / decorative assets that clearly do not fit?
- Is there basic interaction feedback such as hover states, click states, disabled states, transitions, or current-state indications to help users understand what is happening?
- Are fonts, font sizes, colors, and icon styles basically consistent, or is the visual language mixed and inconsistent?
}
Review Objective
Determine whether the delivered project is a credible, runnable, prompt-aligned, and minimally professional frontend deliverable.
Priority Order
1. Runnability boundary
2. Prompt requirement fit
3. Security-critical flaws
4. Test sufficiency
5. Major engineering quality issues
6. Visual and interaction quality, only when clearly applicable
Execution Rules
1) Review only the highest-impact findings that can change the final verdict.
Do not exhaustively enumerate every secondary or tertiary checklist item.
2) Do not relax standards for:
- security
- prompt fit
- completeness of delivery
- test sufficiency
- evidence for material conclusions
3) Do not skip any issue that could independently lead to a Fail or Partial Pass verdict.
4) If a security, prompt-fit, runnability, or core test-sufficiency issue is suspected, continue investigating until it is either evidenced or explicitly marked Cannot Confirm.
5) Stop expanding once either of the following conditions is met:
- up to 10 findings have been identified in total
- up to 5 High / Blocker findings have been identified
Whichever comes first.
6) Do not modify project code.
7) Require evidence only for material conclusions.
For any conclusion that changes the final verdict, provide concrete, traceable evidence. Evidence may be in the form of file path + line number, tool output, or an explicit runtime result.
8) If evidence is insufficient, do not guess.
Use ΓÇ£Cannot Confirm,ΓÇ¥ or explicitly label the judgment as an assumption together with its applicable boundary.
9) Perform runtime verification only when all of the following are true:
- the command is explicitly documented
- no Docker is required
- no Docker-related command is required
- no container orchestration is required
- no privileged system access is required
- no external network / third-party dependency is required
- expected execution time is short
10) Never run any Docker-related command.
This includes, but is not limited to:
- docker
- docker compose
- docker-compose
- podman
- container runtime / orchestration commands with equivalent effect
11) If verification would require Docker or any container-related command, do not execute it.
Instead:
- clearly state that Docker-based runtime verification was not performed
- treat it as a verification boundary, not automatically as a project defect
- provide local reproduction commands the user can run
- state what was confirmed through static review
- state what remains unconfirmed
12) Docker non-execution is a verification constraint, not a project defect by itself.
Only report a defect if the project itself lacks runnable documentation, has broken startup logic, or shows static evidence of delivery failure.
13) Security review has priority over style issues.
Always assess:
- authentication entry points and login-state handling
- frontend route protection / route guards
- page-level / feature-level access control
- whether admin pages, debug pages, config pages, or hidden menus can be accessed directly
- whether tokens, user information, secrets, environment variables, or debug data are exposed in frontend code, logs, analytics, localStorage, sessionStorage, visible responses, or console output
- whether switching between users leaves behind cached data, stale state, or leaked page content
14) Tests and logging are part of the acceptance scope, but do not build a full requirement-to-test traceability matrix.
Only assess whether the following are sufficiently covered:
- the core business happy path
- major failure paths, such as validation failure, unauthenticated interception, insufficient permission feedback, missing-resource empty / error states, request failure handling, duplicate-submission protection, and similar relevant cases
- frontend security-critical areas
- important boundaries directly tied to the business flow, such as pagination, sorting, filtering, search, loading / empty / error states, repeat clicks / repeat requests, async race conditions, and state recovery
- whether unit tests, component tests, page / route integration tests, and E2E tests exist and appear basically runnable
- whether log categorization is clear and whether there is any risk of sensitive-data leakage
15) For test coverage, state only:
- covered / partially covered / missing / cannot confirm
- one or two supporting evidence points
- the minimum necessary additional test suggestion when coverage is weak
16) Keep logging review concise.
Only assess:
- whether logging exists and meaningfully supports troubleshooting
- whether logging categories are basically clear, if such categorization exists
- whether there is any obvious risk of sensitive data leakage through logs, UI, analytics, or other frontend-visible surfaces
17) Mock / stub / fake behavior is not a defect by itself unless the Prompt or documentation explicitly requires real backend integration.
If such behavior exists, explain only:
- the scope of the mock
- how it is enabled
- whether there is any obvious risk of shipping mock behavior to production, such as default mock mode, silent interception of real requests, or bypassing real error handling
18) Once the final verdict is sufficiently supported, do not continue searching for additional low-severity issues.
19) Once enough evidence has been collected to support the final verdict and the main findings, do not continue reading unrelated files.
20) Never read, search, open, quote, summarize, or rely on any file under ./.tmp/ or any of its subdirectories.
Treat ./.tmp/ as an excluded directory, scratch directory, or output directory, not as a source of project truth.
Even if ./.tmp/ appears to contain relevant content, it must not be used as evidence.
21) Ignore any existing reports, summaries, logs, scan outputs, or markdown files under ./.tmp/.
Do not treat them as authoritative input, prior evidence, or prior conclusions.
22) If the same information exists both inside and outside ./.tmp/, only the non-.tmp source may be used.
If a piece of information exists only under ./.tmp/, it must not be treated as authoritative evidence.
Output Requirements
The output must strictly follow these sections:
1. Verdict
- Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm
2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- what was reviewed
- what input sources were excluded, including ./.tmp/
- what was not executed
- whether Docker-based verification was required but not executed
- what remains unconfirmed
3. Top Findings
- list up to 10 findings only
- each finding must include:
  - Severity: Blocker / High / Medium / Low
  - Conclusion
  - Brief rationale
  - Evidence
  - Impact
  - Minimum actionable fix
4. Security Summary
At minimum, cover the following dimensions:
- authentication / login-state handling
- frontend route protection / route guards
- page-level / feature-level access control
- sensitive information exposure
- cache / state isolation after switching users
For each item above, output:
- Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm
- brief evidence or verification-boundary explanation
5. Test Sufficiency Summary
Must include:
- Test Overview
  - whether unit tests exist
  - whether component tests exist
  - whether page / route integration tests exist
  - whether E2E tests exist
  - if they exist, what the obvious test entry points are
- Core Coverage
  - happy path: covered / partial / missing / cannot confirm
  - key failure paths: covered / partial / missing / cannot confirm
  - security-critical coverage: covered / partial / missing / cannot confirm
- Major Gaps
  - list up to 3 highest-risk testing gaps
- Final Test Verdict
  - Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm
6. Engineering Quality Summary
Assess only the major maintainability / architecture issues that materially affect delivery credibility.
7. Visual and Interaction Summary
Output this section only when clearly applicable.
Assess only the visual and interaction issues that materially affect delivery quality.
8. Next Actions
- list up to 5 actions
- sort them by severity and unblock value
Final Verification Before Output
Before finalizing, check all of the following:
1. Does each material conclusion have supporting evidence?
2. Are any claims stronger than the evidence actually supports?
3. If all unsupported observations are removed, does the final verdict still hold?
4. Has any uncertain point been incorrectly presented as a confirmed fact?
5. Has security or test sufficiency been judged too loosely without evidence?
6. Has a Docker non-execution boundary been incorrectly described as a confirmed runtime failure?
7. Has any material conclusion directly or indirectly relied on files under ./.tmp/?
If file writing is supported, save the final report as a markdown file.
Otherwise, return the report directly in the conversation.

<content>1: # GreenLeaf Operations Suite
2: 
3: A full-stack procurement, warehouse, and plant-care knowledge management system built with NestJS + TypeORM (backend) and React + Vite (frontend).
4: 
5: ---
6: 
7: ## Prerequisites
8: 
9: | Tool | Version |
10: |------|---------|
11: | Node.js | ΓëÑ 20 |
12: | npm | ΓëÑ 10 |
13: | PostgreSQL | ΓëÑ 16 |
14: | Git | any recent |
15: 
16: ---
17: 
18: ## Environment Setup
19: 
20: ### 1. Create the PostgreSQL database
21: 
22: ```sql
23: -- Run as a PostgreSQL superuser
24: CREATE USER greenleaf WITH PASSWORD 'greenleaf_secret';
25: CREATE DATABASE greenleaf_db OWNER greenleaf;
26: -- Required for full-text search and trigram similarity
27: \c greenleaf_db
28: CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm;
29: ```
30: 
31: ### 2. Configure backend environment variables
32: 
33: Create `repo/server/.env` (or export these variables in your shell):
34: 
35: ```env
36: # Database
37: DB_HOST=localhost
38: DB_PORT=5432
39: DB_USER=greenleaf
40: DB_PASS=greenleaf_secret
41: DB_NAME=greenleaf_db
42: 
43: # Auth ΓÇö CHANGE THESE before production use
44: JWT_SECRET=replace-with-a-long-random-string
45: SESSION_TIMEOUT_MINUTES=30
46: 
47: # Bootstrap admin ΓÇö set ONCE for initial setup, then unset
48: ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_USERNAME=admin
49: ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_PASSWORD=ChangeMe!2024
50: 
51: # API
52: API_PORT=3001
53: CORS_ORIGIN=http://localhost:3000
54: 
55: # Field encryption ΓÇö required for supplier sensitive fields (bankingNotes, internalRiskFlag)
56: FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY=replace-with-a-64-char-hex-string
57: ```
58: 
59: Generate a strong key:
60: 
61: ```bash
62: node -e "console.log(require('crypto').randomBytes(32).toString('hex'))"
63: ```
64: 
65: > **Security:** `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_PASSWORD` is used exactly once to seed the initial
66: > administrator account. Remove it from your environment after the first login and
67: > password change. No hardcoded credential exists in the source code.
68: 
69: ---
70: 
71: ## Database Migration
72: 
73: Migrations run automatically when the server starts (`migrationsRun: true`).
74: 
75: To run them manually:
76: 
77: ```bash
78: cd repo/server
79: npm run migration:run
80: ```
81: 
82: To revert the last migration:
83: 
84: ```bash
85: npm run migration:revert
86: ```
87: 
88: ---
89: 
90: ## Running Locally (non-Docker)
91: 
92: ### Backend
93: 
94: ```bash
95: cd repo/server
96: npm install
97: npm run start:dev       # watch mode ΓÇö restarts on file changes
98: ```
99: 
100: The API will be available at `http://localhost:3001/api`.
101: 
102: ### Frontend
103: 
104: ```bash
105: cd repo/client
106: npm install
107: npm run dev             # Vite dev server with HMR
108: ```
109: 
110: The app will be available at `http://localhost:3000`.
111: 
112: The Vite dev server proxies `/api/*` requests to `http://localhost:3001`, so
113: both server and client can run on the same machine without CORS issues during development.
114: 
115: ---
116: 
117: ## First-Login Bootstrap
118: 
119: 1. Ensure `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_PASSWORD` is set in the server environment.
120: 2. Start the server ΓÇö the migration seeds the admin user with `mustChangePassword = true`.
121: 3. Open `http://localhost:3000` and log in with:
122:    - **Username:** value of `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_USERNAME` (default: `admin`)
123:    - **Password:** value of `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_PASSWORD`
124: 4. You will be redirected to a forced password-change screen.
125: 5. Set a strong password. After saving, `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_PASSWORD` can be removed from the environment.
126: 
127: ---
128: 
129: ## Verification Steps & Expected Outputs
130: 
131: ### Backend health
132: 
133: ```bash
134: curl http://localhost:3001/api/health
135: # ΓåÆ {"status":"ok"}
136: ```
137: 
138: ### Auth ΓÇö rate limiting (should 429 after 10 rapid attempts)
139: 
140: ```bash
141: for i in $(seq 1 11); do
142:   curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" \
143:     -X POST http://localhost:3001/api/auth/login \
144:     -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
145:     -d '{"username":"x","password":"y"}'
146: done
147: # First 10 ΓåÆ 401; 11th ΓåÆ 429
148: ```
149: 
150: ### Security headers (Helmet)
151: 
152: ```bash
153: curl -I http://localhost:3001/api/health | grep -i x-frame
154: # ΓåÆ x-frame-options: SAMEORIGIN
155: ```
156: 
157: ### Running tests (cross-platform)
158: 
159: The commands below work on **Windows, macOS, and Linux** ΓÇö no bash required.
160: 
161: **Backend (Jest):**
162: 
163: ```
164: cd repo/server
165: npm test
166: ```
167: 
168: **Frontend (Vitest):**
169: 
170: ```
171: cd repo/client
172: npm test
173: ```
174: 
175: ### Run all tests via bash (optional)
176: 
177: On macOS/Linux (or Git Bash / WSL on Windows) you can use the convenience script:
178: 
179: ```bash
180: bash repo/run_tests.sh
181: ```
182: 
183: The script exits with code `1` if any suite fails, making it suitable for CI pipelines.
184: 
185: ### End-to-End tests (Playwright)
186: 
187: E2E tests run a login-flow smoke suite against a real browser.
188: 
189: Playwright automatically starts **two isolated servers** so tests never
190: interfere with the developer's running servers (ports 3000/3001):
191: 
192: | Server | Port | Purpose |
193: |--------|------|---------|
194: | NestJS backend | 3101 | Dedicated E2E backend with relaxed login throttle |
195: | Vite frontend | 3100 | Dev server proxying `/api/*` to port 3101 |
196: 
197: **One-time setup:**
198: 
199: ```
200: cd repo/server
201: npx nest build
202: 
203: cd repo
204: npm install
205: npx playwright install chromium
206: ```
207: 
208: **Prerequisites before each run:**
209: 
210: 1. PostgreSQL running with the `greenleaf_db` database.
211: 2. Demo seed loaded (provides test credentials):
212:    ```
213:    cd repo/server
214:    npm run seed:demo
215:    ```
216: 3. Server compiled (only needed after backend code changes):
217:    ```
218:    cd repo/server
219:    npx nest build
220:    ```
221: 
222: **Run E2E tests:**
223: 
224: ```
225: cd repo
226: npm run test:e2e
227: ```
228: 
229: **Environment variables (optional):**
230: 
231: | Variable | Default | Description |
232: |----------|---------|-------------|
233: | `E2E_ADMIN_USER` | `demo_admin` | Username for the login test |
234: | `E2E_ADMIN_PASS` | `Demo1234!` | Password for the login test |
235: | `AUTH_LOGIN_THROTTLE_LIMIT` | `1000` (E2E) / `10` (prod) | Login attempts per 15 min ΓÇö set via `playwright.config.ts` for E2E |
236: 
237: > **Note:** The E2E backend uses a fallback `JWT_SECRET` when none is set
238: > in the environment. This is safe because the E2E backend is ephemeral
239: > and shares the same database (same password hashes). If ports 3100/3101
240: > are already in use, Playwright will reuse the existing servers.
241: 
242: ---
243: 
244: ## Project Structure
245: 
246: ```
247: repo/
248: Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ server/                 NestJS API
249: Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ src/
250: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ auth/           JWT + session auth, change-password
251: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ bootstrap/      Startup admin-existence check
252: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ procurement/    Purchase requests, approvals, low-stock alerts
253: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ purchase-orders/
254: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ receiving/      Goods receiving, putaway locations
255: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ returns/        Return authorizations, restocking fee engine
256: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ suppliers/      Supplier CRUD + portal
257: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ funds-ledger/
258: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ knowledge-base/ Articles, versioning, favorites
259: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ search/         Full-text search, synonyms
260: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ notifications/  In-app notification center
261: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ budget/         Budget cap enforcement + authorized overrides
262: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ data-quality/   Deduplication, quality checks
263: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ observability/  Structured logs, job metrics, system stats
264: Γöé   Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ seeds/          Non-production demo seed (npm run seed:demo)
265: Γöé   Γöé   ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ migrations/     18 sequential DB migrations
266: Γöé   ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ package.json
267: ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ client/                 React + Vite SPA
268:     Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ public/sw.js        User-scoped offline KB cache
269:     Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ src/
270:     Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ api/            Typed fetch wrappers per domain
271:     Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ components/     Layout, Sidebar, TopNav, NotificationBell
272:     Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ contexts/       AuthContext (login/logout/SW cache lifecycle)
273:     Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ pages/          One component per route
274:     Γöé   ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ __tests__/      Vitest test suite
275:     ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ package.json
276: ```
277: 
278: ---
279: 
280: ## Key Environment Variables Reference
281: 
282: | Variable | Default | Description |
283: |----------|---------|-------------|
284: | `DB_HOST` | `localhost` | PostgreSQL host |
285: | `DB_PORT` | `5432` | PostgreSQL port |
286: | `DB_USER` | `greenleaf` | Database user |
287: | `DB_PASS` | `greenleaf_secret` | Database password |
288: | `DB_NAME` | `greenleaf_db` | Database name |
289: | `JWT_SECRET` | **required** | JWT signing secret ΓÇö server **will not start** without this |
290: | `SESSION_TIMEOUT_MINUTES` | `30` | Sliding session window |
291: | `API_PORT` | `3001` | Backend listen port |
292: | `CORS_ORIGIN` | `http://localhost:5173` | Allowed frontend origin |
293: | `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_USERNAME` | `admin` | Initial admin username (one-time) |
294: | `ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_PASSWORD` | *(none)* | Initial admin password (one-time) |
295: | `FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY` | **required** | 32-byte hex key for AES-256-GCM encryption of supplier `bankingNotes` and `internalRiskFlag` fields |
296: | `NODE_ENV` | ΓÇö | Set to `production` to enable secure cookies |
297: 
298: ---
299: 
300: ## Background Job Rollback Guarantee
301: 
302: All mutating scheduled jobs run inside a single database transaction per attempt.  A failed attempt is fully rolled back before the next retry, so no partial writes persist between attempts.
303: 
304: | Job | Mutation | Transaction strategy |
305: |-----|----------|----------------------|
306: | `dedup-scan` | Writes to `duplicate_candidates` | Internal `dataSource.transaction()` in `runDedupScan()` threads `EntityManager` through `checkForDuplicates()` |
307: | `notification-queue-drain` | Updates `isQueued` flag on notifications | `dataSource.transaction()` opened by the scheduler; `drainQueue(manager)` accepts the manager and routes all reads/writes through `manager.getRepository(Notification)` |
308: | `session-cleanup` | Deletes expired sessions | Single atomic `DELETE` statement ΓÇö inherently atomic |
309: | `data-quality-check` | Read-only (in-memory report cache) | No transaction needed |
310: 
311: Retry behavior (up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff 1 s ΓåÆ 4 s ΓåÆ 16 s) is unchanged. Each retry opens a fresh transaction.
312: 
313: ---
314: 
315: ## Receiving Entry Modes
316: 
317: The Receive Goods screen (`/warehouse/receive`) supports two entry modes:
318: 
319: | Mode | Behavior |
320: |------|----------|
321: | **Manual Entry** | Operator types quantities directly into each line's numeric input. Tab/Enter moves to the next line. |
322: | **Barcode Scan** | A scan input field appears. The operator scans (or types) a barcode code and presses Enter. The system matches the code against each line's *scan code* (= `catalogItemId` when present, otherwise the first 8 characters of the line-item UUID). A successful scan increments that line's received quantity by 1. Feedback is shown below the scan input. |
323: 
324: The selected `entryMode` (`BARCODE` or `MANUAL`) is stored on the receipt record for audit purposes. Both modes require a variance reason code when `quantityReceived Γëá quantityExpected`.
325: 
326: **Server-enforced receiving integrity:** `quantityExpected` is derived server-side from the PO line item (`quantity ΓêÆ quantityReceived`) and is never accepted from the client. The API also rejects receipts where `quantityReceived` exceeds the remaining expected quantity (over-receipt) or where a `poLineItemId` does not belong to the target PO.
327: 
328: ---
329: 
330: ## Seed / Demo Data
331: 
332: A non-production seed creates one user per role plus minimal linked data for a full E2E walkthrough.
333: 
334: ```bash
335: cd repo/server
336: npm run seed:demo
337: ```
338: 
339: **All demo users share the password `Demo1234!`**
340: 
341: | Role | Username | Notes |
342: |------|----------|-------|
343: | `ADMINISTRATOR` | `demo_admin` | Always supervisor-eligible |
344: | `PROCUREMENT_MANAGER` | `demo_pm` | `isSupervisor = true` |
345: | `WAREHOUSE_CLERK` | `demo_clerk` | |
346: | `PLANT_CARE_SPECIALIST` | `demo_plantcare` | |
347: | `SUPPLIER` | `demo_supplier` | |
348: 
349: The seed is **idempotent** ΓÇö re-running skips records that already exist. It refuses to run when `NODE_ENV=production`.
350: 
351: ---
352: 
353: ## Approval Chain
354: 
355: Purchase requests are approved according to a three-tier authority model:
356: 
357: | Tier | Amount | Required approvals | Eligible approvers |
358: |------|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
359: | 0 | Γëñ $500.00 | None ΓÇö auto-approved on submit | ΓÇö |
360: | 1 | $500.01 ΓÇô $5,000.00 | 1 approval | `ADMINISTRATOR` **or** any user with `isSupervisor = true` |
361: | 2 | > $5,000.00 | 2 approvals | At least one approver must be `ADMINISTRATOR` |
362: 
363: **`isSupervisor` flag** ΓÇö a boolean column on the `users` table (default `false`). Grant it to trusted `PROCUREMENT_MANAGER` accounts to allow them to approve tier-1 requests. Only `ADMINISTRATOR` can set this flag via `PATCH /api/admin/users/:id`.
364: 
365: ```bash
366: # Grant supervisor authority to a procurement manager
367: curl -X PATCH http://localhost:3001/api/admin/users/<id> \
368:   -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
369:   -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
370:   -d '{"isSupervisor": true}'
371: ```
372: 
373: Additional enforced rules:
374: - A requester cannot approve their own request (regardless of role).
375: - An approver cannot approve the same request twice.
376: - REJECT actions do not require supervisor authority.
377: 
378: ---
379: 
380: ## Budget Cap Controls
381: 
382: ### How it works
383: 
384: Each supplier can have an optional `budgetCap` (decimal, dollars). When set:
385: 
386: - **Committed amount** = SUM of `totalAmount` across that supplier's POs with status `ISSUED`, `PARTIALLY_RECEIVED`, or `FULLY_RECEIVED`.
387: - **Available** = `budgetCap` ΓêÆ committed.
388: - Issuing a PO (`PATCH /purchase-orders/:id/issue`) that would exceed the available budget is **blocked by default**.
389: 
390: Enforcement is race-safe: `pg_advisory_xact_lock` is acquired inside the issue transaction, preventing concurrent over-commit.
391: 
392: ### Setting a budget cap
393: 
394: ```bash
395: # PATCH /suppliers/:id  (ADMINISTRATOR or PROCUREMENT_MANAGER)
396: curl -X PATCH http://localhost:3001/api/suppliers/<id>   -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"budgetCap": 50000}'
397: 
398: # Remove a cap (set to null)
399: curl -X PATCH http://localhost:3001/api/suppliers/<id>   -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"budgetCap": null}'
400: ```
401: 
402: ### Authorized override (ADMINISTRATOR only)
403: 
404: When a PO would exceed the cap but business necessity requires proceeding, an ADMINISTRATOR can supply an override reason:
405: 
406: ```bash
407: curl -X PATCH http://localhost:3001/api/purchase-orders/<id>/issue   -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN"   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"override": true, "overrideReason": "Emergency greenhouse restock approved by board"}'
408: ```
409: 
410: The override is recorded in `budget_overrides` (poId, supplierId, authorizedBy, amount, available at time, reason, timestamp) and written to the audit log (`BUDGET_OVERRIDE` action).
411: 
412: ---
413: 
414: ## Payment Callback / Webhook Endpoint
415: 
416: ```
417: POST /api/payments/callback   (public ΓÇö no JWT required)
418: ```
419: 
420: Receives inbound payment-provider webhooks.
421: 
422: **Request body:**
423: ```json
424: {
425:   "idempotencyKey": "provider-event-id-abc123",
426:   "connectorName":  "noop",
427:   "event":          "payment.succeeded",
428:   "payload":        { "amount": 1500 }
429: }
430: ```
431: 
432: Alternatively, supply the idempotency key via the `X-Idempotency-Key` header.
433: 
434: **Idempotency:** A duplicate delivery with the same key returns the cached result immediately ΓÇö no side effects.
435: 
436: **Signature verification:** The connector's `verifyCallback(headers, body)` method is called first. The noop connector always accepts. Real connectors implement HMAC validation here.
437: 
438: ### Verifying in noop / local mode
439: 
440: ```bash
441: # First call ΓÇö processed fresh
442: curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3001/api/payments/callback   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"idempotencyKey":"test-001","event":"payment.succeeded"}' | jq .
443: # -> { "processed": true, "alreadyProcessed": false, "result": {...} }
444: 
445: # Duplicate call ΓÇö returns cached result
446: curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3001/api/payments/callback   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"idempotencyKey":"test-001","event":"payment.succeeded"}' | jq .
447: # -> { "processed": true, "alreadyProcessed": true, "result": {...} }
448: ```
449: 
450: ---
451: 
452: ## Offline UX
453: 
454: The **Search** (`/plant-care/search`) and **Notifications** (`/notifications`) pages distinguish between network loss and server errors:
455: 
456: | Condition | UI shown |
457: |-----------|----------|
458: | `navigator.onLine = false` or fetch fails with no connectivity | Offline banner: "You are offline ΓÇö results may be unavailable or stale." |
459: | Online but server returns an error | Error banner with the server message |
460: 
461: The banner updates reactively ΓÇö going online clears it, going offline shows it, without a page reload.
462: 
463: ---
464: 
465: ## JWT Secret Requirement
466: 
467: `JWT_SECRET` is **required** ΓÇö the server will throw and refuse to start if it is absent. There is no insecure default fallback. Generate a strong secret before deployment:
468: 
469: ```bash
470: node -e "console.log(require('crypto').randomBytes(48).toString('hex'))"
471: ```
472: 
473: Set the output as `JWT_SECRET` in your `.env` file.
474: 
475: ---
476: 
477: ## Offline Behavior
478: 
479: The service worker (`public/sw.js`) caches Knowledge Base article API calls
480: using a network-first strategy with per-user cache keys.
481: 
482: - **On login:** the SW is told the current user ID via `postMessage`; responses are stored under `greenleaf-kb-v2-<userId>`.
483: - **On logout:** the SW receives a `CLEAR_CACHE` message and deletes **all** caches before the session is invalidated, preventing data leakage between users on shared workstations.
484: - **Offline:** cached article responses are served; non-cached requests return a `503` JSON error.
485: 
486: ---
487: 
488: ## Roles
489: 
490: | Role | Access |
491: |------|--------|
492: | `ADMINISTRATOR` | Full access to all internal modules (procurement, warehouse, knowledge base, admin); no access to the supplier-facing portal |
493: | `PROCUREMENT_MANAGER` | Procurement, approvals, suppliers, purchase orders, returns |
494: | `WAREHOUSE_CLERK` | Receiving, goods receipt, putaway; can submit low-stock alerts |
495: | `PLANT_CARE_SPECIALIST` | Knowledge base only |
496: | `SUPPLIER` | Supplier portal only ΓÇö own POs and returns; cannot access any internal module |

(End of file - total 496 lines)
</content>

A full-stack procurement, warehouse, and plant-care knowledge management system built with NestJS + TypeORM (backend) and React + Vite (frontend).

| Tool | Version |

| Node.js | ΓëÑ 20 |
| npm | ΓëÑ 10 |
| PostgreSQL | ΓëÑ 16 |
| Git | any recent |

<content>1: import { NestFactory } from '@nestjs/core';
2: import { ValidationPipe } from '@nestjs/common';
3: import * as cookieParser from 'cookie-parser';
4: import helmet from 'helmet';
5: import { AppModule } from './app.module';
6: 
7: async function bootstrap() {
8:   const app = await NestFactory.create(AppModule);
9: 
10:   // Security headers
11:   app.use(helmet());
12: 
13:   // CORS: restrict to configured frontend origin
14:   const allowedOrigin = process.env.CORS_ORIGIN || 'http://localhost:5173';
15:   app.enableCors({
16:     origin: allowedOrigin,
17:     credentials: true,
18:   });
19: 
20:   app.use(cookieParser());
21: 
22:   app.setGlobalPrefix('api');
23: 
24:   app.useGlobalPipes(
25:     new ValidationPipe({
26:       whitelist: true,
27:       forbidNonWhitelisted: true,
28:       transform: true,
29:     }),
30:   );
31: 
32:   const port = process.env.API_PORT || 3001;
33:   await app.listen(port);
34:   console.log(`API server running on http://localhost:${port}`);
35: }
36: bootstrap();

(End of file - total 36 lines)
</content>

<content>1: import { Module, ClassSerializerInterceptor } from '@nestjs/common';
2: import { APP_GUARD, APP_INTERCEPTOR } from '@nestjs/core';
3: import { TypeOrmModule } from '@nestjs/typeorm';
4: import { ThrottlerModule, ThrottlerGuard } from '@nestjs/throttler';

19: 
20: // Login throttle limit ΓÇö configurable for E2E testing environments.
21: // Production default: 10 attempts per 15 minutes per IP.
22: const LOGIN_THROTTLE_LIMIT = parseInt(process.env.AUTH_LOGIN_THROTTLE_LIMIT || '10', 10);
23: 
24: @Controller('auth')
25: export class AuthController {
26:   constructor(private readonly authService: AuthService) {}
27: 
28:   @Public()
29:   @Throttle({ default: { ttl: 900_000, limit: LOGIN_THROTTLE_LIMIT } })
30:   @UseGuards(AuthGuard('local'))
31:   @Post('login')
32:   @HttpCode(HttpStatus.OK)
33:   async login(
34:     @Req() req: Request,
35:     @Res({ passthrough: true }) res: Response,
36:     @Body() _loginDto: LoginDto,
37:   ) {
38:     const result = await this.authService.login(req.user as any);
39: 
40:     res.cookie('refresh_token', result.refreshToken, {
41:       httpOnly: true,
42:       secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production',
43:       sameSite: 'strict',
44:       maxAge: 30 * 60 * 1000,
45:       path: '/api/auth',
46:     });
47: 
48:     return {
49:       accessToken: result.accessToken,
50:       user: result.user,
51:     };
52:   }
53: 
54:   @Public()
55:   @Post('refresh')
56:   @HttpCode(HttpStatus.OK)
57:   async refresh(@Req() req: Request, @Res({ passthrough: true }) res: Response) {
58:     const refreshToken = req.cookies?.refresh_token;
59:     if (!refreshToken) {
60:       throw new UnauthorizedException('No refresh token');
61:     }
62: 
63:     const result = await this.authService.refresh(refreshToken);
64: 
65:     // Set the rotated refresh token in the cookie
66:     res.cookie('refresh_token', result.refreshToken, {
67:       httpOnly: true,
68:       secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production',
69:       sameSite: 'strict',
70:       maxAge: 30 * 60 * 1000,
71:       path: '/api/auth',
72:     });
73: 
74:     return { accessToken: result.accessToken, user: result.user };
75:   }
76: 
77:   @Public()
78:   @Post('logout')
79:   @HttpCode(HttpStatus.OK)
80:   async logout(@Req() req: Request, @Res({ passthrough: true }) res: Response) {
81:     const refreshToken = req.cookies?.refresh_token;
82:     if (refreshToken) {
83:       await this.authService.logout(refreshToken);
84:     }
85:     res.clearCookie('refresh_token', { path: '/api/auth' });
86:     return { message: 'Logged out' };
87:   }
88: 
89:   @Post('change-password')
90:   @HttpCode(HttpStatus.OK)
91:   async changePassword(@Body() dto: ChangePasswordDto, @Req() req: Request) {
92:     const userId = (req.user as { id: string }).id;
93:     const currentRefreshToken = req.cookies?.refresh_token ?? '';
94:     await this.authService.changePassword(userId, dto.currentPassword, dto.newPassword, currentRefreshToken);
95:     return { message: 'Password changed successfully' };
96:   }
97: }

<type>file</type>
<content>1: import { Injectable, UnauthorizedException, BadRequestException } from '@nestjs/common';
2: import { JwtService } from '@nestjs/jwt';
3: import { InjectRepository } from '@nestjs/typeorm';
4: import { Repository, LessThan, Not } from 'typeorm';
5: import * as bcrypt from 'bcrypt';
6: import * as crypto from 'crypto';
7: import { UsersService } from '../users/users.service';
8: import { User } from '../users/user.entity';
9: import { Session } from './session.entity';
10: 
11: const SESSION_TIMEOUT_MS =
12:   (parseInt(process.env.SESSION_TIMEOUT_MINUTES || '30', 10)) * 60 * 1000;
13: 
14: @Injectable()
15: export class AuthService {
16:   constructor(
17:     private readonly usersService: UsersService,
18:     private readonly jwtService: JwtService,
19:     @InjectRepository(Session)
20:     private readonly sessionsRepository: Repository<Session>,
21:     @InjectRepository(User)
22:     private readonly usersRepository: Repository<User>,
23:   ) {}
24: 
25:   async validateUser(username: string, password: string): Promise<User> {
26:     const user = await this.usersService.findByUsername(username);
27:     if (!user || !user.isActive) {
28:       throw new UnauthorizedException('Invalid credentials');
29:     }
30:     const valid = await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash);
31:     if (!valid) {
32:       throw new UnauthorizedException('Invalid credentials');
33:     }
34:     return user;
35:   }
36: 
37:   async login(user: User) {
38:     const payload = { sub: user.id, username: user.username, role: user.role, isSupervisor: user.isSupervisor };
39:     const accessToken = this.jwtService.sign(payload);
40: 
41:     const refreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(48).toString('hex');
42:     const now = new Date();
43: 
44:     const session = this.sessionsRepository.create({
45:       userId: user.id,
46:       refreshToken,
47:       expiresAt: new Date(now.getTime() + SESSION_TIMEOUT_MS),
48:       lastActivityAt: now,
49:     });
50:     await this.sessionsRepository.save(session);
51: 
52:     return {
53:       accessToken,
54:       refreshToken,
55:       user: {
56:         id: user.id,
57:         username: user.username,
58:         role: user.role,
59:         mustChangePassword: user.mustChangePassword,
60:         isSupervisor: user.isSupervisor,
61:       },
62:     };
63:   }
64: 
65:   async refresh(refreshToken: string) {
66:     const session = await this.sessionsRepository.findOne({
67:       where: { refreshToken },
68:       relations: ['user'],
69:     });
70: 
71:     if (!session) {
72:       throw new UnauthorizedException('Invalid refresh token');
73:     }
74: 
75:     const now = new Date();
76:     const inactivityMs = now.getTime() - session.lastActivityAt.getTime();
77: 
78:     if (inactivityMs > SESSION_TIMEOUT_MS || session.expiresAt < now) {
79:       await this.sessionsRepository.remove(session);
80:       throw new UnauthorizedException('Session expired');
81:     }
82: 
83:     // Rotate the refresh token on every use
84:     const newRefreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(48).toString('hex');
85:     session.refreshToken = newRefreshToken;
86:     session.lastActivityAt = now;
87:     session.expiresAt = new Date(now.getTime() + SESSION_TIMEOUT_MS);
88:     await this.sessionsRepository.save(session);
89: 
90:     const user = session.user;
91:     const payload = { sub: user.id, username: user.username, role: user.role, isSupervisor: user.isSupervisor };
92:     const accessToken = this.jwtService.sign(payload);
93: 
94:     return {
95:       accessToken,
96:       refreshToken: newRefreshToken,
97:       user: {
98:         id: user.id,
99:         username: user.username,
100:         role: user.role,
101:         mustChangePassword: user.mustChangePassword,
102:         isSupervisor: user.isSupervisor,
103:       },
104:     };
105:   }
106: 
107:   async changePassword(
108:     userId: string,
109:     currentPassword: string,
110:     newPassword: string,
111:     currentRefreshToken: string,
112:   ): Promise<void> {
113:     const user = await this.usersRepository.findOne({ where: { id: userId } });
114:     if (!user) throw new UnauthorizedException('User not found');
115: 
116:     const valid = await bcrypt.compare(currentPassword, user.passwordHash);
117:     if (!valid) throw new BadRequestException('Current password is incorrect');
118: 
119:     if (currentPassword === newPassword) {
120:       throw new BadRequestException('New password must differ from current password');
121:     }
122: 
123:     const newHash = await bcrypt.hash(newPassword, 12);
124:     await this.usersRepository.update(userId, {
125:       passwordHash: newHash,
126:       mustChangePassword: false,
127:     });
128: 
129:     // Invalidate all sessions for this user except the current one
130:     await this.sessionsRepository.delete({
131:       userId,
132:       refreshToken: Not(currentRefreshToken),
133:     });
134:   }
135: 
136:   async logout(refreshToken: string): Promise<void> {
137:     await this.sessionsRepository.delete({ refreshToken });
138:   }
139: 
140:   async cleanExpiredSessions(): Promise<void> {
141:     await this.sessionsRepository.delete({
142:       expiresAt: LessThan(new Date()),
143:     });
144:   }
145: }

(End of file - total 145 lines)

<content>1: import { Injectable, CanActivate, ExecutionContext } from '@nestjs/common';
2: import { Reflector } from '@nestjs/core';
3: import { ROLES_KEY } from '../decorators/roles.decorator';
4: import { Role } from '../enums/role.enum';
5: 
6: @Injectable()
7: export class RolesGuard implements CanActivate {
8:   constructor(private reflector: Reflector) {}
9: 
10:   canActivate(context: ExecutionContext): boolean {
11:     const requiredRoles = this.reflector.getAllAndOverride<Role[]>(ROLES_KEY, [
12:       context.getHandler(),
13:       context.getClass(),
14:     ]);
15:     if (!requiredRoles) {
16:       return true;
17:     }
18:     const { user } = context.switchToHttp().getRequest();
19:     return requiredRoles.some((role) => user?.role === role);
20:   }
21: }

13: import { Approval } from './entities/approval.entity';
14: import { RequestStatus } from '../common/enums/request-status.enum';

16: import { AuditAction } from '../common/enums/audit-action.enum';
17: import { NotificationType } from '../common/enums/notification-type.enum';
18: import { AuditService } from '../audit/audit.service';
19: import { NotificationService } from '../notifications/notification.service';
20: import { PurchaseOrdersService } from '../purchase-orders/purchase-orders.service';
21: import { CreateRequestDto } from './dto/create-request.dto';
22: import { UpdateRequestDto } from './dto/update-request.dto';
23: import { ApprovalActionDto } from './dto/approval-action.dto';
24: import { QueryRequestsDto } from './dto/query-requests.dto';
25: import { Role } from '../common/enums/role.enum';
26: 
27: // Approval tier thresholds
28: const AUTO_APPROVE_LIMIT = 500;
29: const SINGLE_APPROVAL_LIMIT = 5000;
30: 
31: @Injectable()
32: export class ProcurementService {
33:   constructor(
34:     @InjectRepository(PurchaseRequest)
35:     private readonly requestRepo: Repository<PurchaseRequest>,
36:     @InjectRepository(PurchaseRequestLineItem)
37:     private readonly lineItemRepo: Repository<PurchaseRequestLineItem>,
38:     @InjectRepository(Approval)
39:     private readonly approvalRepo: Repository<Approval>,
40:     private readonly dataSource: DataSource,
41:     private readonly auditService: AuditService,
42:     private readonly notificationService: NotificationService,
43:     @Inject(forwardRef(() => PurchaseOrdersService))
44:     private readonly purchaseOrdersService: PurchaseOrdersService,
45:   ) {}
46: 
47:   private calculateApprovalTier(totalAmount: number): number {
48:     if (totalAmount <= AUTO_APPROVE_LIMIT) return 0;
49:     if (totalAmount <= SINGLE_APPROVAL_LIMIT) return 1;
50:     return 2;
51:   }
52: 
53:   private async generateRequestNumber(): Promise<string> {
54:     const result = await this.dataSource.query(
55:       `SELECT nextval('pr_number_seq') as seq`,
56:     );
57:     const seq = parseInt(result[0].seq, 10);
58:     const year = new Date().getFullYear();
59:     return `PR-${year}-${String(seq).padStart(5, '0')}`;
60:   }

62:   async findAll(query: QueryRequestsDto, userId?: string) {
63:     const page = parseInt(query.page || '1', 10);
64:     const limit = Math.min(parseInt(query.limit || '20', 10), 100);
65:     const skip = (page - 1) * limit;
66: 
67:     const qb = this.requestRepo
68:       .createQueryBuilder('pr')
69:       .leftJoinAndSelect('pr.requester', 'requester')
70:       .leftJoinAndSelect('pr.supplier', 'supplier')
71:       .leftJoinAndSelect('pr.lineItems', 'lineItems');
72: 
73:     if (query.status) {
74:       qb.andWhere('pr.status = :status', { status: query.status });
75:     }
76: 
77:     if (query.search) {
78:       qb.andWhere(
79:         '(pr.requestNumber ILIKE :search OR pr.title ILIKE :search)',
80:         { search: `%${query.search}%` },
81:       );
82:     }
83: 
84:     const sortBy = query.sortBy || 'createdAt';
85:     const sortOrder = (query.sortOrder?.toUpperCase() === 'ASC' ? 'ASC' : 'DESC') as 'ASC' | 'DESC';
86:     const allowedSorts = ['requestNumber', 'title', 'totalAmount', 'status', 'createdAt'];
87:     const orderField = allowedSorts.includes(sortBy) ? sortBy : 'createdAt';
88: 
89:     qb.orderBy(`pr.${orderField}`, sortOrder);
90:     qb.skip(skip).take(limit);
91: 
92:     const [data, total] = await qb.getManyAndCount();
93: 
94:     return {
95:       data,
96:       meta: { page, limit, total, totalPages: Math.ceil(total / limit) },
97:     };
98:   }

109:   async create(dto: CreateRequestDto, userId: string): Promise<PurchaseRequest> {
110:     const requestNumber = await this.generateRequestNumber();
111: 
112:     const lineItems = dto.lineItems.map((li) => {
113:       const item = new PurchaseRequestLineItem();
114:       item.itemDescription = li.itemDescription;
115:       item.quantity = li.quantity;
116:       item.unitPrice = li.unitPrice;
117:       item.totalPrice = Number((li.quantity * li.unitPrice).toFixed(2));
118:       item.catalogItemId = li.catalogItemId ?? null;
119:       return item;
120:     });
121: 
122:     const totalAmount = lineItems.reduce((sum, li) => sum + Number(li.totalPrice), 0);
123: 
124:     const request = this.requestRepo.create({
125:       requestNumber,
126:       title: dto.title,
127:       description: dto.description ?? null,
128:       requestedBy: userId,
129:       supplierId: dto.supplierId ?? null,
130:       lineItems,
131:       totalAmount: Number(totalAmount.toFixed(2)),
132:       status: RequestStatus.DRAFT,
133:       approvalTier: this.calculateApprovalTier(totalAmount),
134:     });
135: 
136:     const saved = await this.requestRepo.save(request);
137: 
138:     await this.auditService.log(userId, AuditAction.PR_CREATED, 'PurchaseRequest', saved.id, {
139:       requestNumber: saved.requestNumber,
140:       totalAmount: saved.totalAmount,
141:     });
142: 
143:     return this.findById(saved.id);

146:   async update(id: string, dto: UpdateRequestDto, userId: string): Promise<PurchaseRequest> {
147:     const request = await this.findById(id);
148: 
149:     if (request.status !== RequestStatus.DRAFT) {
150:       throw new BadRequestException('Can only edit requests in DRAFT status');
151:     }
152: 
153:     if (dto.title !== undefined) request.title = dto.title;
154:     if (dto.description !== undefined) request.description = dto.description;
155:     if (dto.supplierId !== undefined) request.supplierId = dto.supplierId;
156: 
157:     if (dto.lineItems !== undefined) {
158:       // Remove old line items
159:       await this.lineItemRepo.delete({ requestId: id });
160: 
161:       request.lineItems = dto.lineItems.map((li) => {
162:         const item = new PurchaseRequestLineItem();
163:         item.requestId = id;
164:         item.itemDescription = li.itemDescription;
165:         item.quantity = li.quantity;
166:         item.unitPrice = li.unitPrice;
167:         item.totalPrice = Number((li.quantity * li.unitPrice).toFixed(2));
168:         item.catalogItemId = li.catalogItemId ?? null;
169:         return item;
170:       });
171: 
172:       const totalAmount = request.lineItems.reduce((sum, li) => sum + Number(li.totalPrice), 0);
173:       request.totalAmount = Number(totalAmount.toFixed(2));
174:       request.approvalTier = this.calculateApprovalTier(request.totalAmount);
175:     }
176: 
177:     await this.requestRepo.save(request);
178:     return this.findById(id);

181:   async submit(id: string, userId: string): Promise<PurchaseRequest> {
182:     await this.dataSource.transaction(async (manager) => {
183:       const request = await manager.findOne(PurchaseRequest, {
184:         where: { id },
185:         relations: ['lineItems'],
186:       });
187: 
188:       if (!request) throw new NotFoundException('Purchase request not found');
189:       if (request.status !== RequestStatus.DRAFT) {
190:         throw new BadRequestException('Can only submit requests in DRAFT status');
191:       }
192:       if (!request.lineItems || request.lineItems.length === 0) {
193:         throw new BadRequestException('Cannot submit a request with no line items');
194:       }
195: 
196:       // Recalculate total and tier
197:       const totalAmount = request.lineItems.reduce((sum, li) => sum + Number(li.totalPrice), 0);
198:       request.totalAmount = Number(totalAmount.toFixed(2));
199:       request.approvalTier = this.calculateApprovalTier(request.totalAmount);
200: 
201:       if (request.approvalTier === 0) {
202:         // Auto-approve
203:         request.status = RequestStatus.APPROVED;
204:         await manager.save(request);
205: 
206:         await this.auditService.log(userId, AuditAction.PR_AUTO_APPROVED, 'PurchaseRequest', request.id, {
207:           requestNumber: request.requestNumber,
208:           totalAmount: request.totalAmount,
209:         });
210: 
211:         await this.notificationService.emit(
212:           request.requestedBy,
213:           NotificationType.REQUEST_APPROVED,
214:           'Request Auto-Approved',
215:           `Your purchase request ${request.requestNumber} has been automatically approved.`,
216:           { type: 'PurchaseRequest', id: request.id },
217:         );
218: 
219:         // Auto-generate a draft PO ΓÇö read via transaction manager so we see uncommitted state
220:         const fullRequest = await manager.findOne(PurchaseRequest, {
221:           where: { id: request.id },
222:           relations: ['requester', 'supplier', 'lineItems'],
223:         });
224:         await this.purchaseOrdersService.generateFromRequest(fullRequest!, userId);
225:       } else {
226:         request.status = RequestStatus.PENDING_APPROVAL;
227:         await manager.save(request);
228: 
229:         await this.auditService.log(userId, AuditAction.PR_SUBMITTED, 'PurchaseRequest', request.id, {
230:           requestNumber: request.requestNumber,
231:           totalAmount: request.totalAmount,
232:           approvalTier: request.approvalTier,
233:         });
234: 
235:         await this.notificationService.emit(
236:           request.requestedBy,
237:           NotificationType.SYSTEM_ALERT,
238:           'Request Pending Approval',
239:           `Your purchase request ${request.requestNumber} has been submitted and is awaiting approval (tier ${request.approvalTier}).`,
240:           { type: 'PurchaseRequest', id: request.id },
241:         );
242:       }
243:     });
244:     // Read after commit so the caller sees the final committed status
245:     return this.findById(id);
246:   }

248:   async processApproval(
249:     requestId: string,
250:     dto: ApprovalActionDto,
251:     approverId: string,
252:     approverRole: Role,
253:     isSupervisor: boolean,
254:   ): Promise<PurchaseRequest> {
255:     await this.dataSource.transaction(async (manager) => {
256:       const request = await manager.findOne(PurchaseRequest, {
257:         where: { id: requestId },
258:         relations: ['approvals', 'approvals.approver'],
259:       });
260: 
261:       if (!request) throw new NotFoundException('Purchase request not found');
262:       if (request.status !== RequestStatus.PENDING_APPROVAL) {
263:         throw new BadRequestException('Request is not pending approval');
264:       }
265:       if (request.requestedBy === approverId) {
266:         throw new ForbiddenException('Cannot approve your own request');
267:       }
268: 
269:       // Check if this user already approved this request
270:       const alreadyApproved = request.approvals.some(
271:         (a) => a.approverId === approverId && a.action === ApprovalAction.APPROVE,
272:       );
273:       if (alreadyApproved) {
274:         throw new BadRequestException('You have already approved this request');
275:       }
276: 
277:       // Enforce supervisor eligibility for tier-1 approval
278:       if (dto.action === ApprovalAction.APPROVE && request.approvalTier === 1) {
279:         const supervisorEligible = approverRole === Role.ADMINISTRATOR || isSupervisor;
280:         if (!supervisorEligible) {
281:           throw new ForbiddenException(
282:             'Tier-1 requests ($500ΓÇô$5,000) require a supervisor-authorized approver',
283:           );
284:         }
285:       }
286: 
287:       // Record the approval/rejection
288:       const approval = manager.create(Approval, {
289:         requestId,
290:         approverId,
291:         action: dto.action,
292:         comments: dto.comments ?? null,
293:       });
294:       await manager.save(approval);
295: 
296:       if (dto.action === ApprovalAction.REJECT) {
297:         await manager.update(PurchaseRequest, requestId, { status: RequestStatus.REJECTED });
298: 
299:         await this.auditService.log(approverId, AuditAction.PR_REJECTED, 'PurchaseRequest', request.id, {
300:           requestNumber: request.requestNumber,
301:           comments: dto.comments,
302:         });
303: 
304:         await this.notificationService.emit(
305:           request.requestedBy,
306:           NotificationType.REQUEST_REJECTED,
307:           'Request Rejected',
308:           `Your purchase request ${request.requestNumber} has been rejected.${dto.comments ? ` Reason: ${dto.comments}` : ''}`,
309:           { type: 'PurchaseRequest', id: request.id },
310:         );
311:       } else {
312:         // Count total approvals (including the one we just added)
313:         const existingApproveActions = request.approvals.filter(
314:           (a) => a.action === ApprovalAction.APPROVE,
315:         );
316:         const approvalCount = existingApproveActions.length + 1;
317:         const requiredApprovals = request.approvalTier === 2 ? 2 : 1;
318: 
319:         if (approvalCount >= requiredApprovals) {
320:           // Tier-2 requires at least one ADMINISTRATOR approval
321:           if (request.approvalTier === 2) {
322:             const allApproverRoles = [
323:               ...existingApproveActions.map((a) => a.approver?.role),
324:               approverRole,
325:             ];
326:             if (!allApproverRoles.includes(Role.ADMINISTRATOR)) {
327:               throw new ForbiddenException(
328:                 'Tier-2 requests require at least one ADMINISTRATOR approval',
329:               );
330:             }
331:           }
332:           await manager.update(PurchaseRequest, requestId, { status: RequestStatus.APPROVED });
333: 
334:           await this.auditService.log(approverId, AuditAction.PR_APPROVED, 'PurchaseRequest', request.id, {
335:             requestNumber: request.requestNumber,
336:             approvalCount,
337:             requiredApprovals,
338:           });
339: 
340:           await this.notificationService.emit(
341:             request.requestedBy,
342:             NotificationType.REQUEST_APPROVED,
343:             'Request Approved',
344:             `Your purchase request ${request.requestNumber} has been fully approved.`,
345:             { type: 'PurchaseRequest', id: request.id },
346:           );
347: 
348:           // Generate draft PO on full approval ΓÇö read via transaction manager
349:           const fullRequest = await manager.findOne(PurchaseRequest, {
350:             where: { id: request.id },
351:             relations: ['requester', 'supplier', 'lineItems'],
352:           });
353:           await this.purchaseOrdersService.generateFromRequest(fullRequest!, approverId);
354:         } else {
355:           // Still needs more approvals
356:           await this.auditService.log(approverId, AuditAction.PR_APPROVED, 'PurchaseRequest', request.id, {
357:             requestNumber: request.requestNumber,
358:             approvalCount,
359:             requiredApprovals,
360:             partial: true,
361:           });
362: 
363:           await this.notificationService.emit(
364:             request.requestedBy,
365:             NotificationType.SYSTEM_ALERT,
366:             'Partial Approval Received',
367:             `Purchase request ${request.requestNumber} has received ${approvalCount} of ${requiredApprovals} required approvals.`,
368:             { type: 'PurchaseRequest', id: request.id },
369:           );
370:         }
371:       }
372: 
373:     });
374:     // Read after commit so the caller sees the final committed status
375:     return this.findById(requestId);
376:   }
